FULL TEXT OF PREVIOUS CHAPTER (match this prose style, rhythm, and vocabulary — maintain continuity of voice):
Chapter 14: The Terms

The Malhotra Media House boardroom smelled of aged teak and the metallic tang of panic. Vikram stood at the window, his reflection fractured by rain streaking the glass. His hands were steady, but the vein in his temple pulsed like a second heartbeat.

Zara sat at the head of the table, her fingers resting on the mahogany like a queen surveying her chessboard. Priya stood beside her, arms crossed, the golden pendant hidden beneath the collar of her blouse.

Vikram turned. His suit jacket was unbuttoned, his tie loose. “You want to dissolve my studio.”

“No,” Zara said. “I want you to dissolve it yourself. Publicly. Voluntarily.”

Priya unfolded her arms. “In exchange for three things.”

Vikram’s laugh was a dry crack. “You’re not in a position to name terms.”

Priya stepped forward. “I am. Because I’m the one who recorded your confession. The one you made after you lost the gala. The one where you threatened to ruin me if I didn’t walk away.” She pulled out her phone, tapped a button. The recorder played Vikram’s slurred voice: *“She thinks she’s untouchable? I’ll drag her into the gutter with the rest of them. Starting with that whore Kapoor.”*

Vikram’s face darkened. “You were supposed to be an asset.”

“I was never yours,” Priya said.

Zara’s fingers tapped once on the table. “The terms are simple. Withdraw your hostile takeover of ZK Studios. Release Priya’s contract. And surrender the microfilm in your safe to the authorities by dawn.”

Vikram’s jaw worked. “You think I’ll kneel for you?”

Priya leaned in. “I think you’ll kneel for the truth. Because if you don’t, every journalist, every cop, every rival in this town gets a copy of that recording. And your empire will burn slower than you did to others.”

Vikram’s eyes flicked between them. “And if I refuse?”

Zara smiled. It didn’t reach her eyes. “Then I release the footage of your son handing me the pendant. The one you gave him to destroy.” She reached into her bag, pulled out an envelope. Slid it across the table. “Your financials. Every bribe. Every blackmail. The whole ledger.”

Vikram’s breath hitched. His gaze locked onto the envelope like a man staring into an abyss.

Priya’s voice dropped. “Choose.”

The rain outside drummed against the windows. A clock on the wall ticked, slow and deliberate.

Vikram exhaled through his nose. Then he reached for the envelope. Tore it in half. Dropped the pieces onto the table.

“Done,” he said.

Zara didn’t move. “Say it.”

Vikram met her eyes. “I dissolve Malhotra Media. I release Priya Kapoor from all obligations. And I hand over the microfilm.” He picked up a pen, signed a single sheet of paper without reading it. Slid it toward Zara.

She didn’t touch it. “Priya.”

Priya stepped forward, picked up the paper. Folded it. Put it in her pocket. “I’ll make sure it’s delivered.”

Vikram looked at her. Really looked at her. “You’re not what I thought.”

Priya’s smile was razor-thin. “Neither are you.”

Zara stood. “The gala starts in two hours.”

Vikram laughed, bitter. “You’re really going through with it? The reveal? The fairy tale?”

Zara adjusted her blouse. “No. The revolution.”

She turned to leave. Priya followed. Vikram stayed by the window, watching them go.

The door clicked shut behind them.

Priya waited until they were in the elevator before she spoke. “He’ll try to undo this.”

Zara pressed the button for the ground floor. “He can try. But the footage is already encrypted and distributed. The microfilm is on its way to the CBI. And your contract is in my hands.”

Priya’s fingers twitched toward her pocket. “You gave up everything for this.”

Zara watched the numbers light up, one by one. “Not everything.”

The elevator dinged. The doors opened.

Outside, the Diwali lights of Bandra blazed against the evening sky. Traffic hummed, chaotic and alive. Priya’s car waited at the curb, engine running.

Zara turned to her. “Are you coming?”

Priya hesitated. Then she took Zara’s hand.

Not gently. Not politely. She gripped it like a lifeline, like a challenge, like a promise.

Zara’s fingers curled around hers, tight.

They walked to the car together. The doors opened. They got in. The car pulled away.

The city swallowed them whole.

Inside, Priya’s thumb brushed the inside of Zara’s wrist. A pulse. A connection. A secret.

Zara didn’t let go.

calls.response_content:
Chapter 15: The Crown

The ZK Studios lot had been scrubbed clean, painted gold and crimson for Diwali. Banners hung from every lamppost: *Zara & Priya Films presents—The New Era*. The scent of jasmine and fried snacks hung thick in the humid evening air. Zara stepped from the black Mercedes, her heels sinking into fresh gravel. She didn’t pause to adjust her sari, didn’t glance at the crowd behind the velvet rope. The photographers’ flashes were background noise, like distant thunder.

Arjun exited the car on the other side, already scanning the faces in the crowd. “Security’s tight. Media’s vetted. No surprises.”

Priya stepped out last. Her emerald-green lehenga caught the light, the embroidery shifting like liquid as she moved. She didn’t take Zara’s arm. Didn’t offer a smile. Just stood there, chin lifted, letting the cameras eat her up.

Zara adjusted the pendant around her neck. The glass case at the entrance displayed it prominently—engraved with the studio’s founding date: *15 November 2010*. A new date. A new beginning. She caught Priya staring at it.

“You designed the case yourself,” Priya said.

Zara met her eyes. “I wanted them to see.”

Priya’s jaw tightened. “They’ll see what they want to see.”

A reporter broke from the crowd, microphone extended. “Ms. Khan—will you finally reveal yourself tonight?” The crowd leaned in, breath held.

Zara smiled. It wasn’t warm. “Some secrets are better kept in the dark.”

Priya’s fingers curled into her palm, nails biting skin. The motion didn’t go unnoticed. Arjun stepped between them, subtle as a shadow. “The guests are waiting. The stage is set.”

Zara turned toward the main building, where floodlights bathed the red carpet in incandescent gold. Priya fell into step beside her, close enough that their sleeves brushed. Zara didn’t pull away.

Inside, the lobby had been transformed. Marigolds cascaded from chandeliers. A live sitar player plucked soft notes in the corner. Industry heads milled about, sipping champagne, laughing too loud. Vikram wasn’t there. Neither was Karan. Priya’s gaze drifted toward the exit, as if expecting them to appear through the doors.

“He won’t come,” Zara said quietly.

Priya didn’t look at her. “You don’t know that.”

“I know he signed the papers. I know the CBI has his ledger. And I know he’s lost everything but his pride.” Zara plucked two glasses from a passing tray, handed one to Priya. “Drink. You’ll need it.”

Priya took the champagne, downed it in one swallow. The bubbles didn’t soften her expression. “You’re enjoying this.”

Zara sipped hers, slower. “I’m not enjoying anything. I’m winning.”

Priya’s eyes flickered. Something raw passed between them. Guilt? Gratitude? Zara couldn’t tell. Not anymore.

A hand clapped Zara’s shoulder. Rohit Verma, grinning like a man who’d dodged a bullet. “Zara! Heard you locked down Vikram’s secrets. That’s some leverage.” He turned to Priya, bowed slightly. “Priya Kapoor. Unbelievable performance tonight. You’ve redefined stardom.”

Priya nodded, tight-lipped. “Thank you.”

Rohit leaned toward Zara, voice dropping. “Karan Malhotra’s here. Slinking around like a whipped dog. Think he’s trying to make amends?”

Zara’s grip tightened on her glass. “Leave him be.”

Rohit raised his hands in mock surrender. “Just saying. Word is he’s got information. Stuff Vikram didn’t want aired.”

Priya’s fingers twitched. “What kind of information?”

Rohit shrugged. “Family business, I guess. Nothing that concerns us.”

Zara met Priya’s gaze. Held it. Priya didn’t look away.

A gong sounded. A voice boomed over the speakers: “Ladies and gentlemen, please take your seats. The gala will begin in five minutes.”

The crowd shuffled toward the auditorium. Priya moved with them, but Zara caught her wrist. Just for a second. Just enough to anchor her.

Priya turned. “What?”

Zara’s thumb brushed the inside of Priya’s wrist, like she had in the elevator earlier. She didn’t let go. “Stay close.”

Priya’s breath hitched. The air between them thickened. For a heartbeat, Zara wondered if Priya would pull away. But she didn’t. She stepped closer instead, close enough that Zara could smell the jasmine in her hair.

“You’re afraid,” Priya whispered.

“Of what?”

“That it’s not over.”

Zara exhaled through her nose. “It’s never over.”

The sound of a camera shutter snapped behind them. They both turned. A freelance photographer, lens trained on them, grinning like he’d caught a scandal.

Priya stepped back, breaking the moment. “We have to go.”

Zara didn’t release her immediately. Then she dropped her hand. “After you.”

They walked toward the auditorium doors, side by side. Arjun fell in behind, a silent sentinel. The crowd parted for them, murmuring, pointing. Zara could feel the weight of every gaze, every speculation. *Who is the woman behind the pendant? What does she want?*

Priya paused at the auditorium entrance. She looked back at Zara, really looked at her, for the first time in weeks. “You’re wearing the pendant tonight.”

Zara touched it. “Always.”

Priya’s lips parted. As if she wanted to say more. As if she should. But the moment shattered. A stagehand called Priya’s name. She turned, melted into the crowd.

Zara followed.

Inside, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation. Spotlights crisscrossed the stage. The screen behind the podium displayed a looping montage of ZK Studios’ greatest hits. Zara took her seat in the front row. Arjun stood behind her, unobtrusive but watchful.

The lights dimmed.

A single spotlight found Priya as she ascended the stage. The crowd erupted. She didn’t wave. Didn’t smile. She stood at the podium, hands gripping the edges, knuckles white.

The master of ceremonies introduced her. The audience applauded. Priya waited, silent, until the noise faded.

Then she spoke.

“Tonight, we celebrate new beginnings.” Her voice was steady, clear. “A new studio. A new era. And a woman who made it possible.”

Zara felt every eye in the room turn toward her. She didn’t shift. Didn’t flinch. She simply sat there, hands folded in her lap, pendant gleaming under the light.

Priya continued. “But new beginnings require sacrifices. The old ways—the shadows, the secrets, the puppeteers—must burn.”

A hush fell over the crowd. Zara’s pulse kicked up.

Priya reached into her blouse. Pulled out the pendant. Held it up for all to see. “Tonight, we honor the woman who taught me that power isn’t taken. It’s reclaimed.”

Zara’s fingers dug into the armrests.

The pendant swung gently, catching the light like a beacon.

Then Priya turned toward Zara.

And smiled.

Not the practiced smile of a star on stage. Not the polite mask of an ally. But a real smile. A dangerous one.

Zara’s chest tightened.

Priya’s voice dropped, intimate, as if they were the only two people in the room.

“Introducing… Zara Khan.”
